data.body:
PASS 1 review — criterion T1 (Prior-art / novel-architecture justification) [overlay].

VERDICT: PASS — no T1 findings. The plan crosses the novel-architecture + migration bright-lines (a new compaction-surviving provenance set + a provider-agnostic gate, plus a lazy reseed migration), so T1 fires; its prior-art treatment is unusually thorough and tool-corroborated.

AFFIRMATIONS (criteria this chunk satisfies):
- [T1a — prior art considered, not reinvented] The plan explicitly anchors on the in-house `local_label_intent` "ever-seen" predecessor. Verified at src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/local_label_intent.py: that helper scans raw event files (CREATE/EDIT/SNAPSHOT/TAG_DELTA) and is therefore genuinely compaction-vulnerable exactly as the plan claims — compaction collapses the log so the post-boundary ever-seen set loses removed refs. It also names external field tools (Unito/Exalate/FolderSync/Syncthing/ghas-jira-integration) and states what they validate (the intent-gate concept). Prior art is considered before committing.
- [T1b — novelty justified, anti-repackaging / Rule-of-Three] The plan does NOT repackage `local_label_intent`; it diagnoses that pattern's specific failure mode and justifies the divergence (move the provenance set into `compiled_state` so `process_snapshot` restores it across compaction). Verified process_snapshot at src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py:515-519 copies every compiled_state key back — so the "restored for free across compaction" claim holds. Rule-of-Three is explicitly satisfied: parent (tan-elbow-mica/ba2c) + links (wake-inn-parse/908c) + future peers => build the primitive ONCE. This is the right reason to introduce a shared primitive rather than a third bespoke copy.
- [T1c — capability assertions resolved] The two load-bearing assertions check out: (1) "a new event type / field is preserved-and-ignored by older clones (forward-compatible)" is corroborated by the reducer's unknown-event forward-compat path at reducer/_processors.py:649-654 (skipped without error; ticket-compact keeps the file); (2) "process_snapshot restores managed_refs for free" is corroborated by the compiled_state copy loop cited above. No unverified "library supports X" claim remains.

NON-T1 note for the author (out of scope for this pass, recorded so it isn't lost): the plan references "the existing byte-parity guard" in AC4. I could not locate a dedicated SNAPSHOT/compiled_state compaction byte-parity test in-tree (the byte-parity tests I found are alias/CLI-help oriented). That is a verification/AC-grounding concern for a later pass, NOT a prior-art (T1) gap — flagging only so it gets picked up where it belongs.

A clean T1 result here is expected and good: the prior-art trail (one in-house predecessor with a precise failure analysis + named external field, plus Rule-of-Three justification) is exactly what this criterion asks for.
